elements.sent-2411-001: Gravitational lensing by compact, small-scale intervening masses causes frequency-dependent distortions to gravitational-wave events.
elements.sent-2411-002: The optimal signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is often used as a proxy for the detectability of exotic signals in gravitational-wave searches.
elements.sent-2411-003: In reality, the detectability of such signals in a matched-filtering search requires comprehensive consideration of match-filtered SNR, signal-consistency test value, and other factors.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we investigate for the first time the detectability of lensed gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences with a match-filtering search pipeline, GstLAL.
elements.sent-2411-005: Contrary to expectations from the optimal-SNR approximation approach, we show that the strength of a signal (i.e., higher optimal SNR) does not necessarily result in higher detectability.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also demonstrate that lensed gravitational waves with wave optics effects can suffer significantly, from $~90\%$ (unlensed) to $<1\%$ (lensed) detection efficiency, due to downranking by the signal-consistency test values.
elements.sent-2411-007: These findings stress the need to extend current template banks to effectively search for lensed gravitational waves and to reassess current constraints on compact dark matter scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-008: Gravitational wave detection typically relies on searching for signals using templates based on General Relativity (GR)~\cite{Allen:2005fk .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Commonly, templates used in the modelled search consider a simple quasi-circular quadrupole mode waveform~\cite{Usman:2015kfa,LIGOScientific:2018mvr,Nitz:2018imz,Venumadhav:2019tad,Venumadhav:2019lyq,LIGOScientific:2020ibl,Nitz:2020oeq,LIGOScientific:2021usb,KAGRA:2021vkt,Andres:2021vew,Nitz:2021uxj,Nitz:2021zwj,Olsen:2022pin,Mehta:2023zlk,Kumar:2024bfe .}
elements.sent-2411-010: While spin-orbit precessing effect, higher-order mode, and tidal deformability have been considered recenrly~\cite{Wadekar:2023gea,Schmidt:2024hac,Schmidt:2024jbp,Schmidt:2024kxy,Wadekar:2024zdq,Chia:2023tle , there can be other physical effects such as orbital eccentricity, enviromental effects and deviations from GR.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Within the GR framework, gravitational lensing emerges as the dominant mechanism for modifying gravitational waves during their propagation through space~\cite{Deguchi:1986zz,Nakamura:1997sw,Takahashi:2003ix .}
elements.sent-2411-012: A distorted gravitational wave signal, either by lensing or other means, can be problematic to detect with incomplete templates in modelled searches, potentially leading to missed detections or mischaracterizations of the source properties.
elements.sent-2411-013: To determine whether the signals are detectable, one must know the probability that a random noise fluctuation produces the same effects in modelled searches, i.e., the false-alarm-rate, which typical optimal sigal-to-noise ratio (SNR) analyses cannot capture.
elements.sent-2411-014: Gravitational waves can be deflected and distorted by intervening massive objects.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consider the primary gravitational wave sources for the ground-based detector, characterized by wavelength $\lambda_{\rm{GW }\sim 10^6$~m, and a configuration in which the observer and the source are well aligned with the lens.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Then, if the characteristic size of the lens $R_{\rm{Lens }\gg \lambda_{\rm{GW }$, such as galaxies ($R_{\rm{Lens }\sim10^{20}$~m) or galaxy clusters ($R_{\rm{Lens }\sim 10^{23}$~m), gravitational waves follow well-defined paths, and their magnification is achromatic~\cite{Wang:1996as, Dai:2017huk, Ezquiaga:2020gdt .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This leads to distinct and well-separated (in time) copies of the original chirp that differ by a constant phase shift, relative magnification, and a relative time delay.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is the regime known as strong lensing, where multiple images are produced.
elements.sent-2411-019: When $R_{\rm{Lens \sim \lambda_{\rm{GW }$, lenses such as intermediate-mass black holes and dark matter clumps within a galaxy can cause a frequency-dependent modulation of the waveform.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This modulation, involving both interference and diffraction effects, can leave a lensing-induced distortion on a single signal~\cite{Deguchi:1986zz,Nakamura:1997sw,Takahashi:2003ix,Leung:2023lmq .}
elements.sent-2411-021: These distortions hold invaluable information about the possible population of small compact lenses~\cite{Jung:2017flg,Lai:2018rto,Christian:2018vsi,Dai:2018enj,Diego:2019lcd,Diego:2019rzc,Liao:2020hnx,Mishra:2021xzz,Liu:2023ikc,Chakraborty:2024mbr , the caustic structure of different lenses~\cite{Lo:2024wqm , the halo mass function~\cite{Fairbairn:2022xln,Tambalo:2022wlm,Caliskan:2022hbu,Savastano:2023spl , and the cosmic expansion rate~\cite{Cremonese:2021puh,Chen:2024xal .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Understanding them is also key to avoid biases in parameter estimation~\cite{Cao:2014oaa and false claims of violations of general relativity~\cite{Ezquiaga:2020spg,Gupta:2024gun .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Since the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) collaboration \cite{LIGOScientific:2014pky,VIRGO:2014yos first successful detection of gravitational waves in 2015, efforts have been continuously made to search for lensing signatures within gravitational-wave data~\cite{Hannuksela:2019kle,LIGOScientific:2021izm,LIGOScientific:2023bwz,Janquart:2023mvf,Janquart:2024ztv .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: No significant evidence has been found yet.
elements.sent-2411-025: Other efforts pointed that no lensing features have been detected~\cite{McIsaac:2019use, Li:2019osa,Liu:2020par , although Dai et al.~\cite{Dai:2020tpj also highlighted an intriguing candidate that is however astrophysically unlikely.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: These analyses are performed after detecting gravitational waves by different search algorithms designed to search for (unlensed) gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences (CBCs)~\cite{Allen:2005fk,LIGOScientific:2019hgc .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The search algorithms use a pre-defined template bank to detect all the signals from CBCs~\cite{LIGOScientific:2018mvr,LIGOScientific:2020ibl,KAGRA:2021vkt .}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, as we will show in this work, signals can be missed if the lensing distortion is large.
elements.sent-2411-029: This can lead to biases in follow-up lensing analyses.
elements.sent-2411-030: The probability of detecting a lensed signal thus may depend on the source and lens parameters, making it crucial to examine.
elements.sent-2411-031: As the current templates lack gravitational lensing physics, it is fundamental to understand its impact on detectability in matched-filtering searches.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this work, we study for the first time the detectability of lensed signals with state-of-the-art gravitational-wave search pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-033: We perform injection campaigns\footnote{In gravitational-wave searches, to test the search efficiency of a pipeline towards a certain type of gravitational-wave signals, we inject a set of simulated signals in real noise and assess how many signals the pipeline can recover.}
elements.sent-2411-034: This is known as an injection campaign.
elements.sent-2411-035: GstLAL is one of the main pipelines the LVK collaboration uses to search for gravitational-waves signals.
elements.sent-2411-036: We examine the accuracy of the recovered source parameters, matched-filter SNRs, the signal consistency test values, and the false-alarm rates of the injections.
elements.sent-2411-037: This paper is structured as follows: Section~\ref{Section: Search introduces the fundamentals of matched-filtering.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Section~\ref{gstlal describes the essential parts of matched-filtering in GstLAL.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Section~\ref{Section: Lensing introduces gravitational lensing of gravitational waves using the point mass lens model and illustrates the expected effects of lensing on the detectability.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Section~\ref{Section: Methods describes the injection sets and the setup of the matched-filtering searches in our study.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{Section: Results shows the results of the role of lensing and background noises in the detectability of lensed gravitational waves.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section~\ref{Section: Conclusion concludes the results and proposes new strategies for detecting lensed signatures in gravitational waves and underscores the implication of our work.}
elements.sent-2411-043: To access the detectability of gravitational-waves signals, one needs to predict their SNR in matched-filtering searches.
elements.sent-2411-044: Here we introduce the fundamentals of matched-filtering search and discuss the current efforts of detectability studies.
elements.sent-2411-045: The expected gravitational-wave signals from CBC are well characterized by combinations of the binary component parameters.
elements.sent-2411-046: Extrinsic parameters, such as source distances and inclinations, are not included in the template of matched-filtering searches.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the case of the matched-filtering search conducted in this study, which considers the intrinsic parameters, the parameter space is defined by the binary's component masses in the detector frame and spins.
elements.sent-2411-048: The component mass is denoted as $m_{i}$, where $i=\{1,2\}$, and by convention, $m_{1 \geq m_{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The dimensionless spin is given as $\vec{\chi _{i = c \vec{S _{i /Gm^{2 _{i}$, where $c$ is the speed of light, and $G$ is the gravitational constant, and $\vec{S _{i}$ is the spin angular momentum.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The chirp mass determines the phase evolution during the inspiral phase of a binary system to the leading order~\cite{Peters:1964zz,Blanchet:1995ez , \begin{equation}M_{c} = \frac{(m_{1}m_{2})^{3/5}}{(m_{1}+m_{2})^{1/5}}. \label{chirp_mass}\end{equation} The dimensionless spin $\vec{\chi _{i}$ can combine to form an effective inspiral spin $\chi_{\rm{eff }$~\cite{Ajith:2009bn,Santamaria:2010yb , which is the leading contribution for spin in the post-Newtonian expansion and is defined as \begin{equation}\chi_{\rm{eff}} = \frac{(m_{1}\vec{\chi}_{1}+m_{2}\vec{\chi}_{2})\cdot \hat{L}_N }{m_{1}+m_{2}}, \label{effective spin}\end{equation} where $\hat{L _\mathrm{N}$ is the unit vector in the direction of the Newtonian orbital angular momentum.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The spin parameters are assumed to be parallel to $\hat{L _\mathrm{N}$ in our study.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Matched-filtering is a powerful technique that is used to search for gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences and it is the optimal filter for detecting waveforms in stationary Gaussian noise~\cite{Allen:2005fk .}
elements.sent-2411-053: For a given search parameter space (e.g. in the component masses $m_1$-$m_2$ space), a set of gravitational waveforms $h_{T,\; j}$ (also known as ``templates") is precomputed and collectively stored in a ``template bank".
elements.sent-2411-054: The parameters of the templates are $\vec{\theta = \{m1, m2, \chi_1, \chi_2\}$ and in the detector frame~\cite{Krolak:1987ofj .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: To identify a real gravitational-wave signal $h$, we compute the correlation between the template waveform $h_{T,\; j}$ and the data $d$ (which is composed of the background noise and potentially a signal) to construct the signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR) $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-056: In the time domain, this is ~\cite{Allen:2005fk \begin{equation}z_j(t) = x_j(t) + iy_j(t) = 4\int_0^\infty \frac{\tilde{d}(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}}\frac{\tilde{h}^*_{T,\; j}(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}} e^{2 i \pi ft}df, \label{eq:SNR}\end{equation} where $x_j(t)$ and $y_j(t)$ are the real and imaginary parts, respectively, of the complex SNR $z_j(t)$ using the $j$th template $h_{T,\;j}$.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}z_j(t) = x_j(t) + iy_j(t) = 4\int_0^\infty \frac{\tilde{d}(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}}\frac{\tilde{h}^*_{T,\; j}(f)}{\sqrt{S_n(f)}} e^{2 i \pi ft}df, \label{eq:SNR}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: The Fourier transforms of the time-domain signals $d(t)$ and $h_{T,\; j}$ are denoted as $\tilde{d (f)$ and $\tilde{h _{T,\; j (f)$, and $*$ indicates the complex conjugate.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The square root of the noise power spectral density, $\sqrt{S_n(f)}$, is also called the noise amplitude spectral density.
elements.sent-2411-059: The amplitude spectral density is used to ``whiten" both the data and the template waveform.
elements.sent-2411-060: Finally, the factor of $4$ in Eq. \ref{eq:SNR is a normalization constant, such that \begin{equation}1 = 4 \int^{\infty}_{0}\frac{|\tilde{h}_{T,\; j}(f)|}{S_n(f)}df. \label{search1-1}\end{equation} The SNR $\rho(t)$ is defined as the modulus of the complex SNR, i.e. $\rho(t) = |z_j(t)|$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: To quantify the loss in SNR due to an inexact model or to determine the fraction of missed signals in a matched-filter search, we compute the match, which is defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}(h_{T,\; j}, h) = \underset{\phi_0, t_0}{\max} \frac{\langle h_{T,\; j}(\phi_0, \tau_0),h\rangle}{\sqrt{\langle h_{T,\; j}, h_{T,\; j}\rangle \langle h, h\rangle}},\end{equation} where $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$, the noise-weighted inner product, is defined as \begin{equation}\langle a,b\rangle = 4\mathrm{Re}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\tilde{a}^*(f)\tilde{b}(f)}{S_n(f)}df.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}M(h_{T,\; j}, h) = \underset{\phi_0, t_0}{\max} \frac{\langle h_{T,\; j}(\phi_0, \tau_0),h\rangle}{\sqrt{\langle h_{T,\; j}, h_{T,\; j}\rangle \langle h, h\rangle}},\end{equation}

Right: Ratio of the optimal signal-to-noise ratio of lensed ($\rho_{\rm L}$ and unlensed ($\rho_{\rm UL}$) signals for the same $(M_{\rm Lz},y)$ parameter space). Here, we use a spinless binary black hole signal at 125~Mpc with $m_1 = m_2 = 30M_{\odot}$. $\rho_{\rm{L}}/\rho_{\rm{UL}}>1$ holds true for point mass lens model.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In addition, the phase and time shifts, $\phi_0$ and $\tau_0$, maximize the match between the template waveform $h_{T,\; j}$ and the signal $h$.
elements.sent-2411-063: High $\mathcal{M}$ values indicate that $h_{T,\; j}$ and $h$ are well-matched, where a maximum value of $\mathcal{M = 1$ indicates that the signal is exactly described by the template waveform.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In the case where the template waveform exactly matches the signal (i.e. $h = h_{T,\; j}$) and the SNR is optimized with respect to noise realizations, the SNR is called the ``optimal SNR'' $\rho_{\rm opt}$ , and the ``observed SNR'' $\rho_{\rm obs}$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-065: Various methods, each with its own strengths and limitations, have been used to assess gravitational-wave signal detectability.
elements.sent-2411-066: One widely used approach, employed in studies by~\cite{Tiwari:2017ndi,Veske:2021qis,Gerosa:2020pgy , calculates the optimal signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) from a network of detectors, assuming that the same source parameters are recovered in matched-filtering searches.}
elements.sent-2411-067: This method has been applied to evaluate the detectability of gravitational-wave signals with wave optics effects, as seen in works by~\cite{Bondarescu:2022srx,Mishra:2023ddt .}
elements.sent-2411-068: While this assumption isn't strictly valid due to noise fluctuations, it is a useful proxy for assessing detectability, particularly in high SNR scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-069: Although these approaches don't incorporate signal consistency tests, they offer a computationally efficient solution for analyzing millions of signals.
elements.sent-2411-070: Magee el al.~\cite{Magee:2023muf included the signal consistency test to investigate detectability.}
elements.sent-2411-071: While their study did not compute complete likelihood-ratio ranking statistics, it did consider the impact of signal consistency test values on matched-filtering searches, offering valuable insights into this aspect of signal detection.
elements.sent-2411-072: For the most comprehensive analysis of detectability, full injection campaigns are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-073: These campaigns simulate the detection process using search pipelines and simulated gravitational-wave signals.
elements.sent-2411-074: This approach provides detailed information about the injected signals, including matched-filter SNR, signal consistency test values, and final detection statistics.
elements.sent-2411-075: While computationally intensive, injection campaigns offer the most accurate and complete assessment of gravitational-wave signal detectability across various scenarios and detector configurations~\cite{2017PhRvD..95d2001M .}

elements.sent-2411-076: In this paper, we will focus on the matched-filtering search employed in the search pipeline, GstLAL.
elements.sent-2411-077: We will focus on the autocorrealtion based signal consistency-test detection statistic, and the ranking statistic assignment of GstLAL, which will aid our discussion in later sections of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-078: When $\rho(t)$ exceeds a predetermined threshold with $\rho=4$, the search pipeline registers the template parameters as a potential candidate, referred to as a trigger~\cite{2017PhRvD..95d2001M .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Non-Gaussianity and ``glitches" in the data can also produce high SNR triggers that mimic gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-080: GstLAL therefore assigns for each trigger a signal consistency test value $\xi^{2}$, which is based on autocorrelation function.
elements.sent-2411-081: Mathematically, we have, for a given template $h_{T,\; j}$, \begin{equation}\xi_{j}^{2} = \frac{\int_{-\delta t}^{\delta t} \left|z_{j}(t)-z_{j}(0)R_{j}(t)\right|^{2} dt}{\int_{-\delta t}^{\delta t} 2 - 2\left|R_{j}(t)\right|^{2}dt}, \label{search2}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}R_j(t) = \int \frac{|\tilde{h}_{T, \; 2j}(f)|^2 + |\tilde{h}_{T, \; 2j+1}(f)|^2}{S_n(f)} e^{2\pi i f t} df, \label{search2-1}\end{equation} is the expected SNR time series.
elements.sent-2411-082: Here $t=0$ is the time of the global maximum in the actual SNR time series.
elements.sent-2411-083: The tunable parameter $\delta t$ defines the size of the window around the peak time over which to perform the integration~\cite{2017PhRvD..95d2001M .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Deviation from the expected SNR time series will result in larger $\xi^{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Consider the extreme case where the trigger from a glitch is a scaled delta-function $z_j(0)\delta(t)$ at time $t=0$ with amplitude $z_j(0)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: The actual SNR time series will then be identically $0$ everywhere over the integration range except for $t=0$, which differs from the expected SNR time series (i.e. $z_j(0) R_j(t)$) significantly.
elements.sent-2411-087: Essentially, the numerator of the $\xi^2$ will become large.
elements.sent-2411-088: In fact, the larger the SNR of the noise trigger (i.e. the larger the value of $z_j(0)$), the higher the value $\xi^2$ will be.
elements.sent-2411-089: Also, a signal that differs from the template waveforms can potentially impact the $\xi^{2}$ and is not trivial~\cite{Bondarescu:2022srx .}

The lensed waveform has the same impact parameter $y=0.01$. Left: redshifted lens mass $M_{\rm{Lz}} = 10M_{\odot}$ with match $\mathcal{M} = 0.99$. Middle: $M_{\rm{Lz}} = 10^3 M_{\odot}$, $\mathcal{M} = 0.97$. Right: $M_{\rm{Lz}} = 10^5 M_{\odot}$, $\mathcal{M} = 0.7$. Lensing distortion can enlarge the amplitude.}
elements.sent-2411-090: For each trigger, GstLAL assigns a likelihood-ratio ranking statistic based on the detection statistics (e.g. $\rho$, $\xi^2$)~\cite{2017PhRvD..95d2001M,Sachdev:2019vvd,2015arXiv150404632C,Fong:2018elx .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The likelihood ratio is the ratio of probabilities of obtaining the set of detection statistics for a trigger under the signal hypothesis to that under the noise hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-092: Mathematically, it is defined as \begin{equation}L = \frac{P(\vec{D}_H,\vec{O},\vec\rho,\vec\xi^2,\left[\vec{\Delta t}, \vec{\Delta \phi}\right]|\vec\theta,signal)}{P(\vec{D}_H,\vec{O},\vec\rho,\vec\xi^2,\left[\vec{\Delta t}, \vec{\Delta \phi}\right]|\vec\theta,noise)}\cdot\frac{P(\vec\theta|signal)}{P(\vec\theta|noise)}, \label{likelihood_ratio_ranking}\end{equation} where $\vec{D _H$ are the horizon distances of the detectors, $\vec{O}$ is the set of detectors that registered the trigger, $\vec\rho$ are the recorded detector SNRs for the trigger at each participating detector, $\vec\xi^2$ are the autocorrealtion-based consistency test values computed for each of the participating detectors, $\left[\vec{\Delta t , \vec{\Delta \phi \right]$ are the sets of differences in times $\Delta t$ and coalescence phases $\Delta \phi$ measured by the participating detectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: In previous works~\cite{Tiwari:2017ndi,Veske:2021qis,Gerosa:2020pgy , it has been assumed that optimal SNRs can be used as a proxy to determine the detectability of gravitational-wave signals in matched-filtering-based detection pipelines.}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, we remind readers that such an assumption is incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-095: It has been shown that the likelihood ratio provides the most powerful detection statistic at a given false-alarm probability ~\cite{Neyman:1933wgr,Cannon:2015gha .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Matched-filtered SNR will be a good proxy if it scales approximately linearly with the likelihood ratio, which can only be true if the noise in gravitational-wave data is stationary and Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-097: Therefore, a thorough injection study is required to explore the detectability of gravitational-wave signals instead of using matched-filtered SNRs as a proxy.
elements.sent-2411-098: GstLAL then computes the complementary cumulative distribution defined by \begin{equation}C(\ln L^* | noise) = \int_{\ln L^*}^\infty P\left(\ln L | noise\right) d\lnL,\end{equation} which allows us to compute the False-Alarm-Probability (FAP, or the p-value) as the probability that $M$ independent coincident noise-like events ($\{N_1, N_2, ...}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}C(\ln L^* | noise) = \int_{\ln L^*}^\infty P\left(\ln L | noise\right) d\lnL,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-099: The FAP denotes the probability that noise can produce a trigger with likelihood ratio $\mathcal{L}$ larger or equal to the likelihood ratio $\mathcal{L ^*$ of the trigger under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-100: One would expect triggers from real gravitational-wave signals to have higher likelihood ratios.
elements.sent-2411-101: Hence, the lower the FAP, the more likely the candidate is a gravitational wave.
elements.sent-2411-102: A similar quantity known as the False-Alarm-Rate (FAR), is also evaluated for each candidate.
elements.sent-2411-103: FAR is defined as \begin{equation}\label{FAR} FAR = \frac{C(\lnL^*|noise)}{T}\end{equation} where $N$ is the total number of triggers and $T$ is the total observing time.
elements.sent-2411-104: Essentially, FAR reflects the rate of noise producing a trigger with likelihood ratio $\mathcal{L}$ larger or equal to that of the trigger under consideration $\mathcal{L ^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The lower the FAR, the more likely the candidate is a real gravitational-wave signal~\cite{2017PhRvD..95d2001M,Sachdev:2019vvd,2015arXiv150404632C,Fong:2018elx .}
elements.sent-2411-106: The typical FAR threshold to claim a confident GW detection in GstLAL is $3.85\times10^{-7}$ Hz ($1$ in $30$ days).
elements.sent-2411-107: In the following sections, we introduce the theory of gravitational lensing and show how lensing imprints frequency-dependent modulation on the waveforms using the point mass lens model.
elements.sent-2411-108: This modulation includes interference and diffraction, resulting in waveform distortion and amplitude enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-109: We briefly discussed the effects of lensing distortion on detectability in mathced-filtering, using optimal SNR as a proxy.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Theory of Gravitational Lensing of Gravitational Waves
elements.sent-2411-110: Gravitational lensing theory describes how waves propagate in a curved background.
elements.sent-2411-111: When considering the gravitational wave propagation in the weak-field limit, where the Newtonian gravitational potential $U\ll 1$ and and employing the thin-lens approximation, gravitational lensing is well-described by the Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction integral over the whole lens plane in frequency domain~\cite{Deguchi:1986zz,Nakamura:1997sw,Takahashi:2003ix : \begin{equation}F(f, \pmb{y}) = \frac{f}{i}\int d^2\pmb{x} ~ e^{i2\pi f \tau_{\rm d}(\pmb{x}, \pmb{y})} , \label{fres}\end{equation} where $f$ is the frequency of the gravitational waves.}

elements.subsection-2411-006: The Impact of Lensing on Signal Detectability in Matched-filtering Searches: Optimal SNR
elements.sent-2411-125: To estimate how lensing within the given parameter space affects the waveform morphology, we calculate the match between the lensed and intrinsic unlensed waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-126: As shown in the left panel of Figure~\ref{match_rho , the match in the region with high $M_{\rm Lz}$ and low $y$ values is remarkably low.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This occurs because the corresponding lensed signals exhibit distinctive beating patterns due to high magnifications and short time delays, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{PML_WF .}
elements.sent-2411-128: Thus, using unlensed waveforms as templates to analyze the lensed waveforms can lead to inaccurate results.
elements.sent-2411-129: In matched-filtering searches, the detectability of gravitational-wave signals is approximated by observed SNRs that can be estimated using optimal SNR.
elements.sent-2411-130: In real detection scenarios, detector noise fluctuates over time, which can cause a template with different source parameters, $h_{\rm tem}$, to be the best-fit template for recovering the detected gravitational wave waveform, $h_{\rm true}$.
elements.sent-2411-131: As a result, the observed SNR and optimal SNR values can differ from the values obtained in the absence of noise.
elements.sent-2411-132: In addition to noise effects, lensing (i.e., waveform distortion) can further affect the search pipeline's ability to recover the best-fit template, resulting in a non-trivial behavior in the observed and optimal SNR.
elements.sent-2411-133: The optimal SNR assumes that the best-fit template is the same as the signal.
elements.sent-2411-134: A higher optimal SNR value generally indicates greater signal detectability.
elements.sent-2411-135: If a signal is lensed by a point mass in a wave optics regime, it is always magnified, resulting in a higher optimal SNR value.
elements.sent-2411-136: The right panel in Fig.~\ref{match_rho illustrates how optimal SNR values increase depending on the lens parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-137: As expected, pairs with high $M_{\rm Lz}$ and low $y$, corresponding to higher magnifications, lead to higher optimal SNRs for lensed signals.

elements.sent-2411-273: Based on the insights gained from our injection campaigns, we suggest the following strategies for detecting the lensed gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-274: First, matched-filtering searches with a lensed template bank are necessary to probe the lens signatures, especially in the regions of large waveform distortions.
elements.sent-2411-275: Construction of such a template bank requires a new injection campaign that further samples all source parameters, as well as a critical assesment of the computational efficiency of extending the parameter space of the template bank.
elements.sent-2411-276: Second, we suggest that coherent wave burst pipelines can offer an alternative path in detecting lensed gravitational-wave signals due to its model-independent nature~\cite{Drago:2020kic .}
elements.sent-2411-277: Unlike matched-filtering searches, coherent wave burst can identify loud signals without relying on pre-defined waveform templates, making it more likely to capture lensing-induced modifications.
elements.sent-2411-278: Performing follow-up lensing analyses on gravitational wave candidates found only by coherent wave burst pipeline presents an avenue for detecting lensing signatures that may have been overlooked by template-based searches.
elements.sent-2411-279: This approach complements matched-filtering techniques and could provide a more comprehensive view of lensed gravitational wave events.
elements.sent-2411-280: Since the detectability of lensed gravitational waves in matched-filtering searches differs from optimal SNR analyses, there are several consequences for other lensing analyses.
elements.sent-2411-281: First, and foremost, the detectability of lensed gravitational waves in the wave-dominated regime is significantly lower in the unlensed matched-filtering searches compared to the optimal SNR approximation.
elements.sent-2411-282: Any follow-up parameter estimation that probes the wave optics signatures can suffer from significant bias, as they are only looking at signals already in current catalogs, and these will not include highly distorted lensed signals.
elements.sent-2411-283: Properly accounting for these selection bias requires a large-scale injection campaign that further samples all the source and lens parameters to specify the probability of detection in the parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-284: Besides, reassessing the lensing detection forecasts and constraints based on optimal SNR are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-285: Current lensing rate estimations are based on optimal SNR detection thresholds~\cite{Meena:2022unp,Mishra:2021xzz,Meena:2022unp,Mishra:2023ddt .}
elements.sent-2411-286: Although significant lensing distortions are rare, current analyses overestimate the detection rate of lensed gravitational waves.
elements.sent-2411-287: Furthermore, the same applies when setting constraints on the source and lens populations from the (non-)observation of lensing in the gravitational-wave catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-288: This is particularly relevant for compact dark-matter scenarios, which could constitute an effective population of lenses for gravitational waves in the wave-dominated regime.
elements.sent-2411-289: In the past, constraints on those scenarios have been performed based on optimal SNR analyses~\cite{Jung:2017flg,Diego:2019rzc,Liao:2020hnx,Oguri:2020ldf,Urrutia:2021qak,Tambalo:2022wlm,Zhou:2022yeo .}
elements.sent-2411-290: Since these constraints do not correctly include the reduced detectability in matched-filtering searches shown in our work, the current constraints are overoptimistic in some parts of the parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-291: A full injection campaign is required for these re-analysis.
elements.sent-2411-292: Searches of new physics in gravitational-wave observations may require dedicated pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-293: This work showcases a scenario in which traditional optimal SNR analysis deviates significantly from matched-filter searches when the new physics are not included in the template.
elements.sent-2411-294: Focusing on the case of gravitational wave lensing, we also show how missing physics impacts the detectability of recovered source parameters and signal consistency tests.
elements.sent-2411-295: Our methodology is however more general and can be applied to other problems of interest.
elements.sent-2411-296: In the future, we will explore this further, performing other injection campaigns to follow up on how different physics, such as deviation from general relativity, impact the detectability.
elements.sent-2411-297: Such investigation can unveil the complex dynamics of how noises and different physics correlate in matched-filtering searches and, hence, help improve the corresponding detection method.

elements.subsection-2411-016: Theory of gravitational lensing of gravitational waves
elements.sent-2411-358: When considering the gravitational-wave propagation in the weak-field limit, where the Newtonian gravitational potential $U\ll 1$ and employing the thin-lens approximation, gravitational lensing is well described by the Fresnel-Kirchhoff diffraction integral over the whole lens plane in the frequency domain~\cite{Deguchi:1986zz,Nakamura:1997sw,Takahashi:2003ix , \begin{equation}F(f, \pmb{y}) = \frac{f}{i}\int d^2\pmb{x} ~ e^{-i2\pi f \tau_{\rm d}(\pmb{x}, \pmb{y})} , \label{fres}\end{equation} where $f$ is the frequency of the gravitational waves.}

elements.sent-2411-484: Properly accounting for these selection biases requires a large-scale injection campaign that further samples all the source and lens parameters to specify the probability of detection in the parameter estimation.

elements.sent-2411-486: Furthermore, the same applies when setting constraints on the source and lens populations from the (non)observation of lensing in the gravitational-wave catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-487: This is particularly relevant for compact dark matter scenarios, which could constitute an effective population of lenses for gravitational waves in the wave-dominated regime.
elements.sent-2411-488: A full injection campaign is required for these reanalysis.
elements.sent-2411-489: Focusing on the case of gravitational-wave lensing, we also show how missing physics impacts the detectability of recovered source parameters and signal-consistency tests.
elements.sent-2411-490: Our methodology is, however, more general and can be applied to other problems of interest.
